feedback.text: As a citizen of the European Union and a lifelong supporter of video games, I urge the European Commission to include protections in the Digital Fairness Act against the permanent disabling of purchased digital games. This issue overlaps with concerns already covered, including exploitative design and gambling-like mechanics, and deserves proper attention. Payment processors like Visa and Mastercard have pressured platforms such as Steam and Itch.io to remove adult-themed and niche games. These removals are not based on legality but on vague content risk ratings. Games dealing with trauma, LGBTQ+ identity, or mental health have been censored. In some cases, customers lost access to titles they already bought. This gives financial infrastructure control over what developers can publish and what consumers can play. Console makers also charge players for online multiplayer even after the full purchase of a game. Online access is free on PC, but on consoles it requires an extra subscription. This puts basic features behind a paywall and limits access for those who cannot afford recurring fees. Companies once claimed this paid model covered server costs, but that excuse no longer holds. The same firms now earn large profits from microtransactions inside the games themselves. Many modern titles also require an internet connection even for single-player content. This forces users to rely on external servers for basic gameplay. Customers who do not have internet access at a given moment should still be able to play single-player games. That right should be protected for titles without online features. Lootboxes are another concern. These randomized systems closely resemble gambling. Players spend real money hoping to unlock valuable digital items, with odds that are rarely disclosed. Research has linked lootbox spending with problem gambling behavior. Some countries now regulate them as gambling, especially in games available to minors. Age verification systems also raise privacy concerns. Some governments, including the United Kingdom, now require users to prove their age through third-party companies. These systems often ask for sensitive data such as ID documents or biometric scans. This creates serious risks. Users are forced to hand over personal information to private firms with unclear data handling practices. If age verification is required, it should be done without collecting personal information. Privacy must be protected. Hundreds of titles have already been removed with no recourse for paying customers. Many were online only, lacking offline functionality or any preservation plan. Their disappearance undermines consumer rights and ignores the cultural value of games as digital art. I purchased The Crew, developed by Ubisoft and sold to millions. Ubisoft delisted it in December 2023 and shut down its servers in March 2024. The game is now completely unusable, despite being paid for in full. No refund. No replacement. Just gone. It has become common for publishers to withdraw access after profiting from millions of sales. Without regulation this trend will continue. I support adding protections to the Digital Fairness Act that prevent companies from disabling access to paid games. If a publisher shuts down a product, they should offer a working offline version or transfer it to a preservation group. This issue already has wide public support. A European Citizens Initiative focused on digital gaming rights has gained over one million signatures. Its goals match the Acts priorities, especially those targeting unfair business practices and manipulation in digital markets. Video games are more than entertainment. They represent art, storytelling, and community. Their removal weakens digital culture and breaks trust with consumers. Legal safeguards are needed to make sure players do not lose access to the games they paid for.
